title: Learning When and What to Ask: a Hierarchical Reinforcement Learning Framework

paragraphs.text:
Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2022

## - LEARNING WHEN AND WHAT TO ASK: A HIERARCHI CAL REINFORCEMENT LEARNING FRAMEWORK

## **Anonymous authors**

Paper under double-blind review

## ABSTRACT

Reliable AI agents should be mindful of the limits of their knowledge and consult humans when sensing that they do not have sufficient knowledge to make sound decisions. We formulate a hierarchical reinforcement learning framework for learning to decide _when_ to request additional information from humans and _what_ type of information would be helpful to request. Our framework extends partially-observed Markov decision processes (POMDPs) by allowing an agent to interact with an assistant to leverage their knowledge in accomplishing tasks. Results on a simulated human-assisted navigation problem demonstrate the effectiveness of our framework: aided with an interaction policy learned by our method, a navigation policy achieves up to a 7 _×_ improvement in task success rate compared to performing tasks only by itself. We find that the ability to request subgoals enables the agent to generalize effectively to tasks in unseen environments. We analyze benefits and challenges of learning with a hierarchical policy structure and suggest directions for future work.

## 1 INTRODUCTION

Human-agent communication at deployment time has been under-explored in machine learning, where the traditional focus has been on building agents that can accomplish tasks on their own (full autonomy). Nevertheless, enabling an agent to exchange information with humans during its operation can potentially enhance its helpfulness and trustworthiness. The ability to request and interpret human advice would help the agent accomplish tasks beyond its built-in knowledge, while the ability to accurately convey when and why it is about to fail would make the agent safer to use.

In his classical work, Grice (1975) outlines the desired characteristics of cooperative communication, commonly known as the _Gricean maxims of cooperation_ . Among these characteristics are informativeness (the maxim of quantity) and faithfulness (the maxim of quality). Human-agent communication in current work has fallen short in these two aspects. Traditional frameworks like imitation learning and reinforcement learning employ limited communication protocols where the agent and the human can only exchange simple intentions (requesting low-level actions or rewards Torrey & Taylor (2013); Knox & Stone (2009)). More powerful frameworks like (Nguyen & Daum´e III, 2019; Nguyen et al., 2019; Kim et al., 2019) allow the agent to process high-level instructions from humans, but the agent still only requests generic help. Recent work in natural language processing endows the agent with the ability to generate rich natural language utterances (Camburu et al., 2018; Rao & Daum´e III, 2018; De Vries et al., 2017; Das et al., 2017), but the communication is not faithful in the sense that the agent only mirrors human-generated utterances without grounding its communication in self-perception of its (in)capabilities and (un)certainties. Essentially it learns to convey what a human may be concerned about, not what _it_ is concerned about.

This paper presents a hierarchical reinforcement learning framework named HARI ( **H** uman- **A** ssisted **R** einforced **I** nteraction), which supports richer and more faithful human-agent communication. Our framework allows the agent to learn to convey intrinstic needs for specific information and to incorporate diverse types of information from humans to make better decisions. Specifically, the agent in HARI is equipped with three information-seeking intentions: in every step, it can choose to request more information about (i) its current state, (ii) the goal state, or (iii) a subgoal state which, if reached, helps it make progress on the current task. Upon receiving a request, the human can transfer new information to the agent by giving new descriptions of the requested state. These de-

An assistant communicates with the agent and can provide it with information about the environment and the task. Initially (A) it may request more information about the goal, but may not know enough about where it currently is. For example, at location B, due to limited perception, it does not recognize that it is in a living room and stands next to a couch. It can obtain such information from the assistant. If the current task becomes too difficult (like at location C), the agent can require the assistant to provide a simpler subtask which, if accomplished, helps it make progress on the main task. The agent maintains a stack of tasks and always executes the task at the top. When the agent receives a (sub)task, it pushes the (sub)task to the top of the stack. When it wants to stop executing a (sub)task, it pops the (sub)task from the stack (e.g., at location D). At location E, the agent empties the stack and terminates its execution.

scriptions will be incorporated as new inputs to the agent’s decision-making policy. The human thus can transfer any form of information that can be interpreted by the policy (e.g., asking the agent to execute skills that it has learned, giving side information that connects the agent to a situation it is more familiar with). Because the agent’s policy can implement a variety of model architectures and learning algorithms, our framework opens up many possibilities for human-agent communication.

To enable faithful communication, we teach the agent to understand its intrinsic needs by interacting with the human and the environment (rather than imitating human behaviors). By requesting different types of information and observing how much each type of information enhances its decisions, the agent gradually learns to determine which information is most useful to obtain in a given situation. With this capability, at deployment time, it can choose when and what information to ask from the human to improve its task performance. To demonstrate the effectiveness of HARI, we simulate a human-assisted navigation problem where an agent has access to only sparse information about its current state and the goal, and can request additional information about these states. On tasks that take place in previously unseen environments, the ability to ask for help improves the agent’s success rate by 7 _×_ higher compared to performing tasks only by itself. This human-assisted agent even outperforms an agent that always has access to dense information in unseen environments, thanks to the ability to request subgoals. We show that performance of the agent can be further improved by recursively asking for subgoals of subgoals. We discuss limitations of the policy’s model and feature representation, which suggest room for future improvements.

## 3 LEVERAGING HUMAN KNOWLEDGE TO BETTER ACCOMPLISH TASKS

We introduce an _assistant_ into the operation environment, who can provide information about the environment’s states. We assume the agent possesses a pre-learned operation policy _π_ ˆ. This policy serves as the _common ground_ between the agent and the assistant, which is a prerequisite for communication between them to occur. For example, this policy represents a set of basic tasks that agent has mastered and the assistant can ask the agent to perform. In general, the more knowledge encoded in this policy, the more effectively the agent can communicate with and leverage help from the assistant. Our goal is to learn an _interaction policy ψθ_ (parametrized by _θ_ ) that controls how the agent communicates with the assistant to gather additional information. The operation policy _π_ ˆ will be invoked by the interaction policy if the latter decides that the agent does not need new information and wants to take an operating action.

The assistant aids the agent by giving new (current or goal) state descriptions, connecting the agent to situations on which it can make better decisions. Consider an object-finding navigation problem, where a robot has been trained to reliably navigate to the kitchen from the living room of a house. Suppose the robot is then asked to “ _find a mug_ ”, an object that it has never heard of. The assistant can help the robot accomplish this task by giving a more informative goal description “ _find a mug in the kitchen_ ”, relating the current task to the kitchen-finding task that the robot has been familiar with. The robot may also have problems with localization: it knows how to get the kitchen from the living room but it may not realize that it is currently the living room. In this case, giving a currentstate description that specifies this information provides the robot with a useful hint on what actions to take next.
Our framework allows the assistant to convey any form of information that the agent can incorporate into its input. As discussed in §2, the notion of “state description” in our framework is general,

Communication between the agent and the assistant can be flexibly enriched by designing the agent’s operation policy to be able to consume the forms of information of interest (e.g., a policy that takes natural language as input).

**Communication with the Assistant.** The assistant is present all the time and knows the agent’s current state _st_ and the goal state _gt_ . It is specified by two functions: a description function _ρA_ : _S × D →_ ∆( _D_ ) and a subgoal function _ωA_ : _S × S →_ ∆( _S_ ). _ρA_ ( _d[′] | s, d_ ) specifies the probability of giving _d[′]_ as the new description of state _s_ given a current description _d_ . _ωA_ ( _g[′] | s, g_ ) indicates the probability of proposing _g[′]_ as a subgoal given a current state _s_ and a goal state _g_ .

At time step _t_ , the assistant accepts three types of request from the agent:

- (a) CUR: requests a new description of _st_ and receives _d[s] t_ +1 _[∼][ρ][A]_[(] _[· |][ s][t][, d][s] t_[)][;] (b) GOAL: requests a new description of _gt_ and receives _d[g] t_ +1 _[∼][ρ][A]_[ (] _[· |][ g][t][, d] t[g]_[)][;] (c) SUB: requests a description of a subgoal _gt_ +1 and receives _d[g] t_ +1 _[∼][ρ][A]_[ (] _[· |][ g][t]_[+1] _[,][ ∅]_[)][ where] _gt_ +1 _∼ ωA_ ( _· | st, gt_ ) and _∅_ is an empty description.

- **Interaction Policy.** The action space of the interaction policy _ψθ_ consists of five actions: _{_ CUR _,_ GOAL _,_ SUB _,_ DO _,_ DONE _}_ . The first three actions correspond to making the three types of request that the assistants accepts. The remaining two actions are used to traverse in the operation environment: (d) DO: executes the action _a_[do] _t_ ≜ arg max _a∈A_ ˆ _π_ ( _a | b[s] t[, d] t[g]_[)][.][The agent transitions to a new] operation state _st_ +1 _∼ T_ ( _st, a_[do] _t_[)][;]

- (e) DONE: determines that the current goal _gt_ has been reached.[2] If _gt_ is a main goal ( _gt_ = _g_ 1), the task episode ends. If _gt_ is a subgoal ( _gt_ = _g_ 1), the agent may choose a new goal to follow. Our problem formulation leaves it open on what goal should be selected next.

By selecting among these actions, the interaction policy essentially decides _when_ to ask the assistant for additional information, and _what_ types of information to ask for. Our formulation does not specify the input space of the interaction policy, as this space depends on how the agent implements its goal memory (i.e. how it stores and retrieves the subgoals). In the next section, we introduce an instantiation where the agent uses a stack data structure to manage (sub)goals.

## 4 HIERARCHICAL REINFORCEMENT LEARNING FRAMEWORK

In this section, we describe the HARI framework. We first formulate the POMDP environment that the interaction policy acts in, referred to as the _interaction environment_ (§4.1). Our construction employs a _goal stack_ to manage multiple levels of (sub)goals (§4.2). A goal stack stores all the tasks the agent has been assigned but has not yet decided to terminate (by choosing the DONE action). It is updated in every step depending on the taken action. We design a cost function (§4.4) that specifies a trade-off between the cost of taking actions (acting cost) and the cost of not completing a task (task error).

## 4.4 COST FUNCTION

The interaction policy needs to balance between two types of cost: the cost of taking actions (acting cost) and the cost of not completing a task (task error). The acting cost also subsumes the cost of communicating with the assistant because, in reality, such interactions consume time, human effort, and possibly trust. Assuming that the assistant is helpful, acting cost and task error usually conflict with each other; for example, the agent may lower its task error if it is willing to suffer a larger acting cost by increasing the number of requests to the assistant.

We employ a simplified model where all types of cost are non-negative real numbers of the same unit. Making a request of type _a_ is assigned a constant cost _γa_ . The cost of taking the DO action is _c_ ( _st, a_[do] _t_[)][,][the][cost][of][executing][the] _[a]_[do] _t_[action][in][the][operation][environment.][Calling][D][ONE][to] terminate execution of the main goal _g_ 1 incurs a task error _c_ ( _st, a_ done). We exclude the task errors

## 5 LEARNING WHEN AND WHAT TO ASK IN HUMAN-ASSISTED NAVIGATION

**Problem.** We apply HARI to modeling a human-assisted navigation (HAN) problem. In HAN, a human requests an agent to find an object in an indoor environment. Each task request asks the agent to go to a room of type _r_ and find an object of type _o_ (e.g., find a mug in a kitchen). The agent is equipped with a camera and shares its camera view with the human. We assume that the human is sufficiently familiar with the environment that they can recognize the agent’s location by looking at its current view. Before issuing a task request, the human imagines a goal location (but do not reveal it to the agent). We are primarily interested in evaluating success in _goal-finding_ , i.e. whether the agent can arrive at the human’s intended goal location. Even though there could be multiple locations that match a request, the agent only succeeds if it arrives exactly at the chosen goal location. We also determine success in _request-fulfilling_ , where the agent successfully fulfills a request if it navigates to any node that is within two meters of an object that matches the request.

While an agent is performing a task, it may request the human to provide additional information via telecommunication (e.g., a chat app). Specifically, it can ask for a description of its current location (CUR), the goal location (GOAL), or a subgoal location that is on the path from its current location to the goal location (SUB). Detail about how the subgoals are determined is in the Appendix.

**Operation Environment.** We construct the operation environments using the environment graphs provided by the Matterport3D simulator (Anderson et al., 2018). Each environment graph is generated from a 3D model of a house where each node is a location in the house and each edge connects two nearby unobstructed locations. Each operation state _s_ corresponds to a node in the graph. At any time, the agent’s operation action space _A_ consists of traversing to any of the nodes that are adjacent to its current node.

We employ a discrete bag-of-features representation for state descriptions.[3] A bag of features represents the information that the agent extracts from the raw input that the agent perceives (e.g., an image, a language sentence). Working with this intermediate input allows us to easily vary the type and amount of information given to the agent. Specifically, we simulate two settings of descriptions: _dense_ and _sparse_ . At evaluation time, the agent perceives sparse descriptions and request the assistant for dense descriptions. A dense description of a current location contains the room name at the location, and the features of _M_ objects restricted to be within _δ_ meters of the location. The features of each object consists of (i) its name, (ii) horizontal and vertical angles (relative to the current viewpoint), and (iii) distance (in meters) from the object to the current location. A dense description of a goal follows the same representation scheme. In the sparse setting, the current-location description does not include the room name. Moreover, we remove the features of objects that are not in the top 100 most frequent objects, emulating an imperfect object detector module. The sparse goal description (the task request) has only features of the target object and the room name where the object is located at. Especially, if a subgoal location is adjacent or coincides with the agent’s current location, instead of describing room and object features, the human specifies the ground-truth action to go to the subgoal (an action is specified by its horizontal and vertical angles, and travel distance).[4]

**Experimental Procedure.** We conduct our experiments in three phases. In the _pre-training_ phase, we learn an operation policy _π_ ˆ with dense descriptions of the current location and the goal. In the _training_ phase, the agent perceives a sparse description of its current location and is given a sparse initial goal description. We use advantage actor-critic (Mnih et al., 2016) to learn an interaction policy _ψθ_ that controls how the agent communicates with the human and navigates in an environment.

3While our representation of state descriptions simplifies the object/room detection problem for the agent, it does not necessarily make the navigation problem easier than with image input, as images may contain information that is not captured by our representation (e.g., object shapes and colors, visualization of paths). 4Here, we emulate a practical scenario where if a destination is visible in the current view, to save effort, a human would concisely tell an agent what to do rather than giving a verbose description of the destination.

The human always returns dense descriptions. The interaction policy is trained in environments that are previously seen as well as unseen during pre-training. Finally, in the _evaluation_ phase, the interaction policy is tested on three conditions: seen environment and target object type but starting from a new room (UNSEENSTR), seen environment but new target object type (UNSEENOBJ), and new environment (UNSEENENV). The pre-trained operation policy _π_ ˆ is fixed during the training and evaluation phases. We create 82,104 examples for pre-training, 65,133 for training, and approximately 2,000 for each validation or test set. Details about the training procedure and the dataset are included in the Appendix.

## 6 RESULTS AND ANALYSES

**Settings.** In our main experiments, we set: the cost of taking a CUR, GOAL, SUB, or DO action to be 0.01 (we will consider other settings subsequently), the cost of calling DONE to terminate the main goal (i.e. task error) equal the (unweighted) length of the shortest-path from the agent’s location to the goal, and the goal stack’s size ( _L_ ) to be 2. We compare our RL-learned interaction policy with a rule-based baseline that first takes the GOAL action and then randomly selects actions. In each episode, we enforce that the rule-based policy can take at most _⌊Xa⌋_ + _y_ actions of type _a_ , where _y ∼_ Bernoulli( _Xa −⌊Xa⌋_ ) and _Xa_ is a constant. We tune each _Xa_ on the validation sets so that the rule-based policy has the same average count of each action as the RL-learned policy. To prevent early termination, we enforce that the rule-based policy cannot take more DONE actions than SUB actions unless when its SUB action’s budget is exhausted. We also construct a skyline where the interaction policy is also learned by RL but with an operation policy that executes subgoals perfectly. As discussed in §5, we are primarily interested in goal-finding success rate and will refer to this metric briefly as success rate.

**Main Results (Table 1).** To inspect the potential benefits of asking for additional information, we compute how much the operation policy _π_ ˆ improves when it is supplied with dense information about the current and/or goal states. As seen, success rate of the operation policy is lifted dramatically when both the current-state and goal descriptions are dense ( _∼_ 2 _×_ increase on UNSEENSTR, _∼_ 5 _×_ on UNSEENOBJ, and _∼_ 3 _×_ on UNSEENENV). We find that dense information about the current state is more helpful on UNSEENSTR, while dense information about the goal is more valuable on UNSEENOBJ and UNSEENENV. This is reasonable because on UNSEENSTR, the agent has been trained to find similar goals. In contrast, the initial goal descriptions in UNSEENOBJ and UNSEENENV are completely new to the agent, thus gathering more information about them is necessary.

Aided by our RL-learned interaction policy, the agent observes a substantial _∼_ 2 _×_ increase in success rate on UNSEENSTR, _∼_ 5 _×_ on UNSEENOBJ, and _∼_ 7 _×_ on UNSEENENV, compared to when performing tasks using only the operation policy. In unseen environments, with its capability of requesting subgoals, the agent impressively doubles the success rate of the operation policy that has access to dense descriptions. Encoding uncertainty in the current-state description (e.g., Finkel et al. (2006); Nguyen & O’Connor (2015)) is a plausible future direction for tackling this issue. Finally, results obtained by replacing the learned operation policy with one that behaves optimally on subgoals shows that further improving performance of the operation policy on shortdistance goals would effectively enhance the agent’s performance on long-distance goals.

**Behavior of the RL-Learned Interaction Policy.** Figure 2a characterizes behaviors of the RLlearned interaction policy in three evaluation conditions. We expect that tasks in UNSEENSTR are the easiest and those in UNSEENENV are the hardest. As the difficulty of the evaluation condition increases, the interaction policy issues more CUR, SUB, and DO actions. The average number of GOAL actions does not vary, showing that the interaction policy has correctly learned that making more than one goal-clarifying request is unnecessary. Figure 2b illustrates the distribution of each action along the length of an episode in the validation UNSEENENV dataset. The GOAL action, if taken, is always taken only once and immediately in the first step. The number of CUR actions gradually decreases over time. The agent makes most SUB requests in the middle of an episode, after its has attempted but failed to accomplish the main goals. We observe similar patterns on the other two validation sets.

**Effects of Varying Action Cost.** As mentioned, we assign the same cost to each CUR, GOAL, SUB, or DO action. Figure 3a demonstrates the effects of changing this cost on the success rate of the agent. Setting the cost equal to 0.5 makes it too costly to take any action, inducing a policy that always calls DONE in the first step and thus fails on all tasks. Overall, the success rate of the agent rises as we reduce the action cost. The increase in success rate is most visible in UNSEENENV and least visible in UNSEENSTR. Figure 3b provides more insights. As the action cost decreases, we observe a growth in the number of SUB and DO actions taken by the interaction policy. Meanwhile, the numbers of CUR and GOAL actions are mostly static. Since requesting subgoals is more helpful in unseen environments than in seen environments, the increase in the number of SUB actions leads the more visible boost in success rate on UNSEENENV tasks.

## 7 RELATED WORK AND CONCLUSION

**Transfer Learning in Reinforcement Learning.** Various frameworks have been proposed to model knowledge transfer from a more capable agent to a novice one (Da Silva & Costa, 2019). Torrey & Taylor (2013) introduce the action-advising framework where a learner strategically requests reference actions from a teacher. Da Silva et al. (2020) investigate uncertainty-based strategies for deciding when to request in this framework. In an agent-to-agent setting, (Da Silva et al., 2017; Zimmer et al., 2014; Omidshafiei et al., 2019) focus on learning a teaching policy in addition to an advice-requesting policy. An important assumption in these papers is that the teacher must share a common action space with the learner. More recent frameworks (Kim et al., 2019; Nguyen et al., 2019; Nguyen & Daum´e III, 2019) relax this assumption by allowing the teacher to specify high-level subgoals instead of low-level actions. HARI can be viewed as a strict extension of these frameworks. It allows the human to specify not only subgoals, but also any additional information about the current state and the goal that agent can interpret. Moreover, HARI equips the agent with _multiple_ communication intentions and teaches it to select the most useful intention to convey in a given situation. Another line of work employs standard RL communication protocol, where the human can only transfer knowledge through numerical scores or categorical feedback (Knox & Stone, 2009; Judah et al., 2010; Peng et al., 2016; Griffith et al., 2013). Maclin & Shavlik (1996) propose a framework where the human advises the agent using a domain-specific language, specifying rules that can be incorporated into the agent’s model. In contrast, HARI operates with a black-box agent model. Sumers et al. (2020) extract features from various types of language feedback to construct a reward function for reinforcement learning. We instead focus on deployment-time communication and directly incorporate the human feedback as input to agent’s operation policy.

**Task-Oriented Dialog and Generating Natural Language Questions.** HARI models a taskoriented dialog problem. Many variants of this problem requires the agent to compose specific questions (De Vries et al., 2017; Das et al., 2017; Thomason et al., 2020). The dominant approach in these problems is to mimic pre-collected human utterances. As discussed previously, naively mirroring human external behavior cannot enable agents to understand the limits of their knowledge. We teach the agent to understand its intrinsic needs through interaction with the human and the environment rather than through imitation of human behaviors. Another related line of work concerns generating natural language explanations of model decisions (Camburu et al., 2018; Hendricks et al., 2016; Rajani et al., 2019).

In summary, this paper presents a general POMDP framework for modeling human-agent communication. While we demonstrate this framework on a simplified navigation problem, our framework can theoretically capture richer types of human-agent communication. Hence, an important empirical question is how well our formulation generalizes to richer environments with more complex interactions and state spaces (Shridhar et al., 2020). Enhancing the sample efficiency of the learning policy by exploiting the hierarchical policy structure is an exciting future direction. Furthermore, techniques for generating faithful explanations (Kumar & Talukdar, 2020; Madsen et al., 2021) can be applied to enhance the specificity of the generated questions.

coarse__glm-4.6.label: Coarse (glm-4.6)
coarse__glm-4.6.overall_feedback:
The paper presents a hierarchical reinforcement learning framework (HARI) for learning when and what to ask human assistants. While the framework is well-motivated and the navigation experiments show promising results (up to 7x improvement), several critical issues undermine the paper's contributions. The evaluation relies on overly simplistic assumptions about human behavior, lacks proper baseline comparisons, and fails to demonstrate scalability or generalization beyond the narrow navigation setting.

The paper's core idea of learning to request specific types of information through a hierarchical policy is valuable and timely. The framework elegantly separates 'when to ask' from 'what to ask' decisions using a goal stack. However, the experimental validation is weakened by unrealistic assumptions about human assistance, inadequate baseline comparisons, and limited analysis of the framework's scalability and robustness.

Overview issues:

- Unrealistic Human Assistant Model: The paper assumes the human assistant is omniscient, always available, and provides perfectly accurate information (Section 5). The assistant functions $ho_A$ and $\ω_A$ are deterministic and optimal, which does not reflect real-world human limitations such as cognitive load, varying expertise, or potential errors. This simplification makes it impossible to assess whether the learned interaction policies would be robust to imperfect or inconsistent human responses. The framework should be tested with noisy or biased human models to demonstrate practical applicability.

- Inadequate Baseline Comparisons: The evaluation compares only against a weak rule-based baseline (Section 6) that randomly selects actions after an initial GOAL request. Missing are comparisons to: (1) non-hierarchical RL approaches that could learn the same policies end-to-end, (2) uncertainty-based querying strategies common in active learning literature, and (3) standard POMDP solvers adapted for this setting. Without these comparisons, it's unclear whether the hierarchical structure is essential or whether simpler approaches could achieve similar performance. The 7x improvement claim is only meaningful relative to the sparse-description baseline, not against competitive methods.

- Constant Query Cost Assumption: The cost function assigns the same constant cost (0.01) to all query types (CUR, GOAL, SUB) regardless of context or information type (Section 4.4). This ignores that different queries impose different cognitive burdens on humans and that context matters (e.g., asking for location information is easier than requesting complex subgoals). Figure 3 shows varying action costs affects behavior, but the paper never explores realistic cost structures where query costs depend on the information type or human state. This limits the framework's claimed ability to balance task performance against human effort.

- Limited Scalability Analysis: The goal stack size is limited to L=3 in experiments (Table 2), with minimal analysis of how performance scales with deeper hierarchies. The paper shows success improves with larger stacks in unseen environments but declines in seen environments, attributing this to overestimation in the critic model. However, there's no systematic study of: (1) how performance degrades as task complexity increases, (2) whether the framework can handle longer-term dependencies beyond 3 levels, or (3) computational costs of maintaining deeper stacks. This raises concerns about the framework's applicability to complex real-world tasks requiring deeper reasoning.

- Insufficient Generalization Evaluation: While the paper tests on unseen environments, objects, and starting positions, these variations are within the same navigation domain and environment type (Matterport3D houses). There is no evaluation of: (1) generalization to entirely different task domains beyond navigation, (2) robustness to different types of human assistance (e.g., textual vs. visual feedback), or (3) performance with different operation policies. The claim that the framework "opens up many possibilities for human-agent communication" (Section 3) is not substantiated by demonstrating flexibility beyond the narrow navigation setting.

- No demonstration of framework on concrete model: The paper presents the Hari framework at an abstract level but never computes its key quantities for a fully specified model. Readers need to see how the belief states, goal stack transitions, and cost function work in practice. The paper should include at least one worked example using a simple navigation graph (e.g., a 5-room grid world) where we can trace through an episode: show the initial belief state, what happens when the agent requests a subgoal, how the stack updates, and how costs accumulate. This would make the hierarchical decomposition concrete and help readers understand the mechanics before seeing the large-scale simulation results.

- Missing sim-to-real validation of human model: The paper claims the framework supports richer human-agent communication but only evaluates with an idealized human assistant that always provides perfect information. To demonstrate practical relevance, the paper should show how the learned interaction policy performs when the human assistant makes errors or has limitations. Specifically, they should test with: (1) noisy descriptions where the assistant occasionally misidentifies objects or rooms, (2) delayed responses to model cognitive load, and (3) varying expertise levels. This would show whether the agent learns to be robust to imperfect assistance—a critical requirement for real-world deployment.

- No guidance on implementing the operation policy: The framework assumes a pre-trained operation policy $\\(\\hat{\\pi}\\)$ but provides no details on how to obtain or implement this crucial component. The paper should discuss: (1) the architecture used for the operation policy in their experiments, (2) how to train it on dense descriptions, (3) what features it expects as input, and (4) how to adapt it for new domains. Without this implementation guidance, other researchers cannot reproduce the framework or apply it to their problems. The paper treats the operation policy as a black box, yet its quality directly affects the interaction policy's performance.

- Unexplored trade-off analysis for query costs: The paper sets all query costs to 0.01 and only varies them uniformly in Figure 3, but real-world applications require understanding how different query types impose different burdens. The paper should analyze the trade-offs when: (1) CUR requests are cheaper than SUB requests (reflecting that location information is easier to provide), (2) costs depend on context (e.g., queries about familiar objects cost less), and (3) costs accumulate over time to model human fatigue. This analysis would demonstrate the framework's ability to balance task performance against realistic human effort constraints, which is central to its claimed contribution.

Recommendation: Major revision. The framework has merit but the experimental validation is insufficient. The unrealistic human model and lack of proper baselines make it difficult to assess the true contribution. The paper needs substantial additional experiments to demonstrate practical applicability and comparative advantage.

Revision targets:

- Implement and compare against stronger baselines including non-hierarchical RL approaches and uncertainty-based querying strategies

- Add experiments with noisy or biased human assistant models to test robustness to imperfect information

- Explore realistic query cost structures where costs vary by information type and context

- Conduct systematic scalability analysis with deeper goal stacks and more complex tasks

- Demonstrate generalization to at least one different task domain beyond navigation

- State-transition function $\bar{T}=T_{s}\cdot T_{G}$ where $T_{s}:\mathcal{S}\times\mathcal{D}\times\bar{\mathcal{A}}\rightarrow\Delta(\mathcal{S}\times\mathcal{D})$ and $T_{G}:\mathcal{G}_{L}\times\bar{\mathcal{A}}\rightarrow\Delta(\mathcal{G}
comments.explanation: The notation $\bar{T}=T_{s}\cdot T_{G}$ is mathematically undefined because $T_{s}$ and $T_{G}$ have different domains and codomains. The product operator "$\cdot$" between functions is not defined when their domains differ. For a proper factorization, $\bar{T}$ should be defined as $\bar{T}(\bar{s},\bar{a}) = T_{s}(s,d^{s},\bar{a}) \times T_{G}(G,\bar{a})$ where $\bar{s}=(s,d^{s},G)$. Rewrite the state-transition function definition as "$\bar{T}:\bar{\mathcal{S}}\times\bar{\mathcal{A}}\rightarrow\Delta(\bar{\mathcal{S}})$ where for $\bar{s}=(s,d^{s},G)$, $\bar{T}(\bar{s},\bar{a}) = T_{s}(s,d^{s},\bar{a}) \times T_{G}(G,\bar{a})$" because the current notation is mathematically undefined.

comments.title: Goal action update logic contradicts description
comments.quote:
G_{t}.{\tt update}(a)=\begin{cases}G_{t}.{\tt pop()},{\tt push}(g_{t},d_{t+1}^{g})&\text{if }a={\tt Goal},\\
G_{t}.{\tt push}(g_{t+1},d_{t+1}^{g})&\text{if }a={\tt Sub},\\
G_{t}.{\tt pop()}&\text{if }a={\tt Done},\\
G_{t}&\text{otherwise}\end{cases}
comments.explanation: The Goal action case contains a logical error. The expression 'G_{t}.pop(),push(g_{t},d_{t+1}^{g})' suggests two operations, but this contradicts the textual description that Goal action 'replaces the top goal description with d_{t+1}^{g}'. Replacing a description should not involve popping the entire goal and pushing it back - it should only modify the description component of the top goal. The current implementation would incorrectly remove and re-add the entire goal tuple instead of just updating its description. Rewrite the Goal case as 'G_{t}.replace_top_description(d_{t+1}^{g})' or explicitly show the description replacement: 'G_{t} with top goal description updated to d_{t+1}^{g}' because the current implementation doesn't match the described behavior.

comments.title: Cost function domain mismatch with state space
comments.quote: Cost function $\bar{c}:(\mathcal{S}\times\mathcal{G}_{L})\times\bar{\mathcal{A}}\rightarrow\mathbb{R}$
comments.explanation: The cost function domain $(\mathcal{S}\times\mathcal{G}_{L})\times\bar{\mathcal{A}}$ is inconsistent with the state space definition $\bar{\mathcal{S}}=\mathcal{S}\times\mathcal{D}\times\mathcal{G}_{L}$. The cost function should take the full interaction state $\bar{s}=(s,d^{s},G)\in\bar{\mathcal{S}}$ as input, but the specified domain omits the description component $d^{s}\in\mathcal{D}$. This creates a mismatch where the cost function cannot access the state description that is part of the interaction state. Rewrite the cost function domain as "$\bar{c}:\bar{\mathcal{S}}\times\bar{\mathcal{A}}\rightarrow\mathbb{R}$" because the current specification is inconsistent with the state space definition.

comments.title: Undefined belief state space
comments.quote: A belief state $\bar{b}_{t}$ summarizes a partial execution $(\bar{s}_{1},\bar{a}_{1},\cdots,\bar{s}_{t})$. We formally define the interaction policy as $\psi_{\theta}:\mathcal{B}\rightarrow\Delta(\mathcal{A})$, where $\mathcal{B}$ is the set of all interaction belief states.
comments.explanation: The set $\mathcal{B}$ of all interaction belief states is referenced but never formally defined. In a POMDP, a belief state is typically a probability distribution over the state space $\bar{\mathcal{S}}$, so $\mathcal{B}$ should be $\Delta(\bar{\mathcal{S}})$. However, the text states that $\bar{b}_{t}$ "summarizes a partial execution" without specifying how this summarization maps to a probability distribution. Add "$\mathcal{B}=\Delta(\bar{\mathcal{S}})$, where each belief state $\bar{b}_{t}$ is a probability distribution over $\bar{\mathcal{S}}$ conditioned on the execution history $(\bar{s}_{1},\bar{a}_{1},\cdots,\bar{s}_{t})$" after the first sentence to properly define the belief state space.

comments.title: Inconsistent goal-description stack definition
comments.quote: Description space $\bar{\mathcal{D}}=\mathcal{D}\times\mathcal{G}_{L}^{d}$ where $\mathcal{G}_{L}^{d}$ is the set of all goal-description stacks of size $L$.
comments.explanation: The definition of $\mathcal{G}_{L}^{d}$ as "the set of all goal-description stacks of size $L$" conflicts with the earlier definition of $\mathcal{G}_{L}$ as "the set of all goal stacks containing at most $L$ elements". The inconsistency is that $\mathcal{G}_{L}$ allows stacks with fewer than $L$ elements ("at most $L$"), while $\mathcal{G}_{L}^{d}$ is defined with exactly $L$ elements ("of size $L$"). This creates a mismatch between the observable descriptions and the underlying goal states. Rewrite "$\mathcal{G}_{L}^{d}$ is the set of all goal-description stacks of size $L$" as "$\mathcal{G}_{L}^{d}$ is the set of all goal-description stacks containing at most $L$ elements" to maintain consistency with $\mathcal{G}_{L}$.

comments.title: Contradiction in GOAL action behavior claim
comments.quote: The interaction policy is trained in environments that are previously seen as well as unseen during pre-training. Finally, in the evaluation phase, the interaction policy is tested on three conditions: seen environment and target object type but starting from a new room (UNSEENSTR), seen environment but new target object type (UNSEENOBJ), and new environment (UNSEENENV).
comments.explanation: This description contradicts the claim in Section 6 that "The GOAL action, if taken, is always taken only once and immediately in the first step." If the interaction policy is trained on both seen and unseen environments during pre-training, then tested on unseen environments, it's unclear how the policy could learn to always take GOAL immediately in the first step across all these different conditions. The training procedure described here suggests the policy would encounter varying levels of uncertainty, which should lead to more adaptive GOAL-taking behavior rather than a fixed first-step pattern. The authors should clarify whether the GOAL-always-first behavior emerges from training or is enforced as a constraint, and reconcile this with the mixed seen/unseen training described.

comments.title: Inconsistent success rate trend in Table 2
comments.quote: Table 2: Success rates and numbers of actions taken with different stack sizes (on validation). Larger stack sizes significantly aid success rates in unseen environments, but not in seen environments.
comments.explanation: The text claims 'Larger stack sizes significantly aid success rates in unseen environments' but Table 2 shows UNSEENSTR success rate decreases from 92.2% to 83.2% as stack size increases. The benefit is specific to UNSEENENV (12.5% → 33.5%). The general claim about 'unseen environments' is too broad. Rewrite 'Larger stack sizes significantly aid success rates in unseen environments, but not in seen environments' as 'Larger stack sizes significantly aid success rates in UNSEENENV, but hurt performance in UNSEENSTR while having mixed effects on UNSEENOBJ'.

comments.title: Mathematical inconsistency in action budget formula
comments.quote: In each episode, we enforce that the rule-based policy can take at most  $\lfloor X_{a}\rfloor +y$  actions of type  $a$ , where  $y\sim \mathrm{Bernoulli}(X_a - \lfloor X_a\rfloor)$  and  $X_{a}$  is a constant.
comments.explanation: The formulation is confusing because it uses the fractional part as a Bernoulli parameter. While mathematically correct that the expected budget equals $X_a$, the expression is unnecessarily opaque. A clearer formulation would be: 'the budget is $\lfloor X_a\rfloor$ with probability $1 - (X_a - \lfloor X_a\rfloor)$ and $\lfloor X_a\rfloor + 1$ with probability $X_a - \lfloor X_a\rfloor$'. Replace the quoted text with 'In each episode, we enforce that the rule-based policy can take at most $\lfloor X_a\rfloor$ or $\lfloor X_a\rfloor + 1$ actions of type $a$, where the budget is $\lfloor X_a\rfloor + 1$ with probability $X_a - \lfloor X_a\rfloor$ and $\lfloor X_a\rfloor$ otherwise, ensuring the expected budget equals $X_a$'.

comments.title: Undefined empty description handling
comments.quote: 3. Sub: requests a description of a subgoal $g_{t+1}$ and receives $d^{g}_{t+1}\sim\rho_{A}\left(\cdot\mid g_{t+1},\emptyset\right)$ where $g_{t+1}\sim\omega_{A}\left(\cdot\mid s_{t},g_{t}\right)$ and $\emptyset$ is an empty description.
comments.explanation: The function $\rho_{A}:\mathcal{S}\times\mathcal{D}\rightarrow\Delta(\mathcal{D})$ is defined to take a state and a description as inputs. However, the Sub request passes $\emptyset$ (empty description) as the second argument. The paper does not specify whether $\emptyset \in \mathcal{D}$ or how $\rho_{A}$ handles this special case. This needs clarification to ensure the function is well-defined. Add a sentence after the $\rho_{A}$ definition stating "We assume $\emptyset \in \mathcal{D}$ and define $\rho_{A}(\cdot\mid s,\emptyset)$ as the prior distribution over descriptions when no context is available" to address this gap.

comments.title: Undefined behavior for empty stack Done action
comments.quote: The Done action pops the top (sub)task from the stack.
comments.explanation: The Done action is defined to pop the top task, but there is no specification of what happens when Done is called on an empty stack (after the main goal is completed). This could lead to undefined behavior or errors in the implementation. The transition function $T_G$ would be undefined for this case since $G_{t}.pop()$ on an empty stack is not defined. Add a condition: 'if $a={\tt Done}$ and stack is not empty: $G_{t}.pop()$, otherwise: $G_{t}$' because the current definition does not handle the edge case of completing the main goal.

comments.title: Missing condition for description transition
comments.quote: The description $d_{t}^{s}$ may vary when the agent moves to a new operation state (by taking the Do action) or requests a new description of $s_{t}$ (by taking the Cur action)
comments.explanation: The explanatory text suggests descriptions should vary when moving to a new operation state, but equation (5) for Do actions uses $\rho(d_{t+1}^{s}\mid s_{t+1})$ which only depends on the new state. This formulation doesn't capture the semantic change that should occur when transitioning between states. The description transition for Do actions should depend on both the old and new states to reflect the change in context. Modify equation (5) for the Do case to $\rho(d_{t+1}^{s}\mid s_{t}, s_{t+1})$ to properly condition on the state transition.

comments.title: Undefined function $T$ in state transition
comments.quote:
\[ P(s_{t+1}\mid s_{t},\bar{a}_{t})=\begin{cases}T\left(s_{t+1}\mid s_{t},a_{t}^{\text{do}}\right)&\text{if }\bar{a}_{t}={\tt Do},\\
\mathbb{1}\{s_{t+1}=s_{t}\}&\text{otherwise}\end{cases} \]
comments.explanation: The function $T(s_{t+1}\mid s_{t},a_{t}^{\text{do}})$ appears without definition. While this likely represents the underlying environment transition function, it should be explicitly defined or referenced. Add a sentence after equation (4) stating: 'Here $T(s_{t+1}\mid s_{t},a_{t}^{\text{do}})$ denotes the environment's state transition function, which maps the current state and action to a probability distribution over next states.'

comments.title: Undefined function $\rho_A$ in description transition
comments.quote:
\[ P(d_{t+1}^{s}\mid d_{t}^{s},s_{t+1},\bar{a}_{t})=\begin{cases}\rho(d_{t+1}^{s}\mid s_{t+1})&\text{if }\bar{a}_{t}={\tt Do},\\
\rho_{A}(d_{t+1}^{s}\mid s_{t+1},d_{t}^{s})&\text{if }\bar{a}_{t}={\tt Cur},\\
\mathbb{1}\{d_{t+1}^{s}=d_{t}^{s}\}&\text{otherwise}\end{cases} \]
comments.explanation: The function $\rho_A(d_{t+1}^{s}\mid s_{t+1},d_{t}^{s})$ appears without definition in equation (5). While $\rho$ is used for Do actions, $\rho_A$ for Cur actions is not explained. This function likely represents the description generation policy when the agent requests clarification, but it needs to be defined. Add a sentence before equation (5) stating: 'The function $\rho_A(d_{t+1}^{s}\mid s_{t+1},d_{t}^{s})$ represents the description generation policy when the agent takes the Cur action, producing a new description based on both the current state and previous description.'

comments.title: Inconsistent notation in Sub request
comments.quote: 3. Sub: requests a description of a subgoal $g_{t+1}$ and receives $d^{g}_{t+1}\sim\rho_{A}\left(\cdot\mid g_{t+1},\emptyset\right)$ where $g_{t+1}\sim\omega_{A}\left(\cdot\mid s_{t},g_{t}\right)$ and $\emptyset$ is an empty description.
comments.explanation: The notation inconsistency is valid. The text defines $d^{s}_{t+1}$ for state descriptions (Cur request) and $d^{g}_{t+1}$ for goal descriptions (Goal request). For subgoal descriptions, using $d^{g}_{t+1}$ creates ambiguity since subgoals are intermediate states, not goals. The notation should be $d^{s}_{t+1}$ to maintain consistency with state descriptions, or a new notation like $d^{sub}_{t+1}$ to explicitly distinguish subgoal descriptions. Rewrite "receives $d^{g}_{t+1}\sim\rho_{A}\left(\cdot\mid g_{t+1},\emptyset\right)$" as "receives $d^{s}_{t+1}\sim\rho_{A}\left(\cdot\mid g_{t+1},\emptyset\right)$" because subgoal descriptions should use consistent notation with state descriptions, not goal descriptions.

coarse__qwen3-235b-a22b-2507.label: Coarse (qwen3-235b-a22b-2507)
coarse__qwen3-235b-a22b-2507.overall_feedback:
The paper presents a conceptually compelling hierarchical reinforcement learning framework for enabling agents to learn when and what to ask humans during task execution. It makes a valuable contribution by formalizing human interaction as a structured, goal-managed process within a POMDP extension, and demonstrates non-trivial gains in success rate on navigation tasks, particularly in unseen environments. The integration of subgoal requests into the interaction policy is a notable design choice that supports generalization.

Overview issues:

- Unclear credit assignment between interaction and operation policies: The paper trains the interaction policy ψ_θ using rewards derived from task success, while the operation policy π̂ is fixed. However, the success of the interaction policy depends entirely on the competence of π̂ in acting on refined state and goal descriptions. This creates a credit assignment problem: poor performance could stem from either a suboptimal interaction policy or an inadequate operation policy, but the training setup does not disentangle these. For example, if the agent fails to reach a subgoal, it is unclear whether the fault lies in ψ_θ’s decision to request the subgoal or in π̂’s inability to execute it. The skyline experiment (perfect navigation on subgoals) acknowledges this issue but does not resolve it in the main setup. A more robust design would include ablation studies that vary π̂’s quality or jointly train both policies with clear reward attribution.

- Unrealistic assumption of always-available and perfectly informative assistant: The assistant is modeled as omniscient and always responsive, providing dense, accurate descriptions on demand. This assumption is critical but not justified: in real human-in-the-loop settings, humans are not always available, may provide incomplete or noisy information, and may not understand the agent’s state. The framework’s performance hinges on high-quality feedback, yet there is no evaluation under partial observability of the assistant’s knowledge or communication delays. This undermines the practical applicability of the approach. The paper should include experiments with noisy or delayed responses, or at least discuss how such limitations would affect policy behavior.

- Inadequate operationalization and justification of the cost function: The cost function assigns a fixed small cost (0.01) to each CUR, GOAL, SUB, and DO action, while task error is set to the shortest-path distance. This arbitrary scaling makes the trade-off between communication cost and task completion unclear. For instance, requesting a subgoal (SUB) incurs the same action cost as a local movement (DO), despite likely requiring more human effort. The results in Figure 3 show that success rate drops sharply as action cost increases, but this sensitivity suggests the policy is brittle rather than robust. The cost parameters should be grounded in empirical estimates of human effort or time, and their impact should be analyzed more systematically, rather than treated as hyperparameters.

- Lack of analysis on hierarchical policy decoupling and training stability: The framework assumes clean separation between the high-level interaction policy and the low-level operation policy, but provides no evidence that this decoupling improves learning efficiency or stability. In hierarchical RL, poor coordination between levels can lead to vanishing gradients or misaligned objectives. The paper does not compare against a flat RL baseline that directly outputs primitive actions and queries, nor does it measure sample complexity. Without such a comparison, it is unclear whether the hierarchical structure is necessary or beneficial beyond the specific task decomposition. A direct ablation removing hierarchy while preserving query actions would strengthen the claim.

- Overstated generalization claims due to narrow evaluation scope: The paper claims the agent generalizes to unseen environments, but the evaluation is limited to variations of navigation tasks within the same domain (Matterport3D). The operation policy is pre-trained on dense descriptions, and the interaction policy is trained and tested on similar task types (find object in room). There is no evidence that the framework generalizes to qualitatively different tasks or domains. The claim that HARI 'theoretically captures richer types of communication' is speculative without empirical support. To support generalization claims, the authors should test on a broader set of tasks or include zero-shot transfer experiments across domains.

- Missing discussion on goal stack limitations and stack depth trade-offs: The paper observes that increasing stack size from 2 to 3 improves performance in unseen environments but harms it in seen ones, attributing this to overuse of subgoal requests. However, it does not analyze why the critic overestimates V-values for SUB actions or propose mechanisms to regulate stack depth. The goal stack mechanism is central to the framework, yet there is no discussion of potential failure modes such as infinite recursion, stack overflow, or conflict between subgoals. The lack of dynamic control over stack depth weakens the framework’s robustness. The authors should either propose a solution (e.g., adaptive depth) or acknowledge this as a critical limitation.

- No ablation of hierarchical structure: The paper claims the hierarchical structure enables richer communication but does not compare against a flat RL agent that selects both navigation and query actions in a single policy. Without this comparison, it is unclear whether the hierarchy improves sample efficiency, reduces action space complexity, or is simply an architectural choice with no measurable benefit. Readers will want to know if the performance gains come from the hierarchical design or just from allowing information requests. The authors should implement a flat baseline that maps belief states directly to {Cur, Goal, Sub, Do, Done} actions and report its success rate and sample complexity on the same tasks.

- Missing analysis of information gain per query type: The framework distinguishes three query types but does not quantify how much each contributes to task success. A key question is whether requesting subgoals dominates performance gains or if Cur/Goal requests are also critical. The paper should compute, for successful trajectories, the average reduction in shortest-path distance or uncertainty (e.g., belief entropy) following each query type. This would validate the design of multiple intentions and show that the agent learns to pick high-value queries, not just more queries.

- No simulation with noisy or delayed human feedback: All experiments assume the assistant provides perfect, immediate responses. Yet in real deployment, human feedback is often delayed or imprecise. The claim of improved real-world applicability is weakened without testing under such conditions. The authors should simulate scenarios where Cur/Goal/Sub requests return noisy descriptions or succeed with probability <1, and measure how success rate degrades. This would show whether the policy is robust to realistic interaction dynamics.

- Unverified claim of 7× improvement over full autonomy: The abstract claims a 7× improvement in unseen environments, but Table 1 shows only a 6.6× increase (19.8% vs 3.0%). The discrepancy needs clarification. More importantly, the comparison is against sparse descriptions, not full autonomy. To justify the claim, the authors must either correct the number or define 'full autonomy' explicitly as the sparse-input baseline and confirm the calculation. Otherwise, the headline result appears overstated.

Recommendation: major revision

Revision targets:

- Conduct ablation studies comparing the hierarchical policy against a flat RL baseline that includes query actions, to demonstrate the necessity and benefits of the hierarchical structure.

- Introduce and evaluate the framework under realistic assumptions, such as noisy, delayed, or incomplete human feedback, to assess robustness.

- Revise the cost function with empirically grounded cost values and provide a sensitivity analysis that justifies the chosen trade-offs between communication and task completion.

- Analyze and address the credit assignment issue between the interaction and operation policies, possibly through joint training or more detailed error attribution.

- Expand evaluation to include zero-shot transfer across task types or domains to support generalization claims.

comments.title: Inaccurate 7× improvement claim in abstract and text
comments.quote: On tasks that take place in previously unseen environments, the ability to ask for help improves the agent's success rate by  $7 \times$  higher compared to performing tasks only by itself.
comments.explanation: The claim of a '7× improvement' is inconsistent with the data in Table 1, which reports a success rate of 19.8% for the RL-learned interaction policy versus 3.0% for the sparse baseline in unseen environments, yielding a 6.6× improvement (19.8 / 3.0 ≈ 6.6). While the phrase 'up to' allows some flexibility, the largest improvement reported does not reach 7×. This overstatement risks misleading readers about the magnitude of the gain, especially as it appears in the abstract and introduction as a headline result. The multiplier should reflect the actual empirical result or be explicitly qualified with the exact values.

comments.title: Unjustified exclusion of subgoal task errors from cost function
comments.explanation: The justification that the interaction policy is only evaluated on the main goal does not suffice to omit task error costs for subgoals. While the final objective is main goal completion, the absence of penalties for failed or unproductive subgoal attempts removes a critical incentive for the agent to issue meaningful requests. This could lead to frivolous or self-defeating SUB actions that consume resources without progress, distorting learning dynamics. The impact of failed subgoals is only indirectly captured through acting costs, but this may not be sufficient to prevent exploitative behavior. The paper should either include an explicit subgoal error term or provide a stronger justification, such as reward shaping or curriculum design, for its omission.

comments.title: Costs lack dimensional consistency and commensurability
comments.quote: We employ a simplified model where all types of cost are non-negative real numbers of the same unit. Making a request of type $a$ is assigned a constant cost $\gamma_{a}$. The cost of taking the Do action is $c(s_{t},a_{t}^{\text{do}})$, the cost of executing the $a_{t}^{\text{do}}$ action in the operation environment. Calling Done to terminate execution of the main goal $g_{1}$ incurs a task error $c(s_{t},a_{\text{done}})$.
comments.explanation: The claim that all costs are 'of the same unit' contradicts their definitions. The Do action cost $c(s_t, a_t^{\text{do}})$ represents operational expenditure (e.g., time or energy), while the task error $c(s_t, a_{\text{done}})$ is defined as shortest-path distance—having units of length. These quantities cannot be meaningfully summed without normalization or scaling coefficients. The model assumes additivity for optimization, but this requires explicit alignment of units. The paper should acknowledge this by stating that costs are normalized or scaled by domain-specific factors to ensure commensurability, rather than asserting they are inherently of the same unit.

comments.title: GOAL action update logic is ill-defined due to stack mutation
comments.quote: G_{t}.\text{update}(a)=\begin{cases}G_{t}.\text{pop}().\text{push}(g_{t},d_{t+1}^{g})&\text{if }a=\text{GOAL},
comments.explanation: The GOAL action is described as replacing the top goal's description, but the update rule first pops the top element $(g_t, d_t^g)$ from the stack and then pushes back $(g_t, d_{t+1}^g)$. This requires that $g_t$ is preserved after being removed, which is not guaranteed by a standard stack interface. The expression $G_t.\text{pop}().\text{push}(g_t, d_{t+1}^g)$ is only valid if $g_t$ is retained in the agent's context prior to the update. A correct implementation would either use an in-place update (e.g., replace_top_description) or explicitly state that $g_t$ is cached before popping. The current formulation risks undefined behavior and misrepresents the data structure's semantics.

comments.title: No mechanism prevents invalid stack operations at boundaries
comments.quote: The Done action pops the top (sub)task from the stack.
comments.explanation: The update function uses pop and push operations without defining behavior for edge cases. If the stack is empty, popping is undefined. If the stack is full (size $L$), pushing a new subgoal must either fail or evict an element, but no mechanism is specified. The transition model assumes $G_{t+1} = G_t.\text{update}(\bar{a}_t)$ is always defined, but this is not ensured for $G_t = \emptyset$ or $|G_t| = L$. A valid POMDP requires transitions to be defined for all states. The paper should either restrict action availability (e.g., disable Done on empty stack, disable SUB on full stack) or define default behaviors for these cases.

comments.title: Cur action incorrectly conditions on next state
comments.quote:
P(d_{t+1}^{s}\mid d_{t}^{s},s_{t+1},\bar{a}_{t})=\begin{cases}\rho(d_{t+1}^{s}\mid s_{t+1})&\text{if }\bar{a}_{t}={\tt Do},\\
\rho_{A}(d_{t+1}^{s}\mid s_{t+1},d_{t}^{s})&\text{if }\bar{a}_{t}={\tt Cur},
comments.explanation: For the Cur action, the description update conditions on $s_{t+1}$, but equation (4) states that $s_{t+1} = s_t$ when $\bar{a}_t \neq \text{Do}$. While $s_{t+1} = s_t$ deterministically, referencing $s_{t+1}$ misleadingly suggests a state transition has occurred. The assistant provides a new description of the current state $s_t$, so the natural conditioning should be on $s_t$, not $s_{t+1}$. This notational choice obscures the causal structure. The second case should be rewritten as $\rho_A(d_{t+1}^s \mid s_t, d_t^s)$ to reflect that the update is based on the current, unchanging state.

comments.title: State-transition factorization assumes unjustified independence
comments.explanation: The factorization assumes $d_{t+1}^s$ is conditionally independent of $s_t$ given $s_{t+1}$, but this is not justified. For example, if the description update depends on the direction or magnitude of state change (e.g., movement relative to landmarks), then $d_{t+1}^s$ could depend on both $s_t$ and $s_{t+1}$. The model assumes the full transition is captured in $s_{t+1}$, but without further assumptions on $\rho$ or $\rho_A$, this independence may not hold. The paper should either justify this assumption or define $T_s$ as a joint model without assuming factorization.

comments.title: Interaction policy output space mismatch
comments.quote: We formally define the interaction policy as $\psi_{\theta}:\mathcal{B}\rightarrow\Delta(\mathcal{A})$, where $\mathcal{B}$ is the set of all interaction belief states.
comments.explanation: The interaction policy $\psi_\theta$ is defined to output over $\mathcal{A}$, the primitive action space of the operation environment. However, $\psi_\theta$ selects high-level actions from $\bar{\mathcal{A}} = \{\text{Cur}, \text{Goal}, \text{Sub}, \text{Do}, \text{Done}\}$, which is distinct from $\mathcal{A}$. The current definition incorrectly suggests $\psi_\theta$ outputs low-level actions, creating a formal inconsistency. It should be redefined as $\psi_\theta : \mathcal{B} \rightarrow \Delta(\bar{\mathcal{A}})$ to align with the interaction environment's action space.

comments.title: No penalty for repeated or premature Done actions
comments.quote: Calling Done to terminate execution of the main goal $g_{1}$ incurs a task error $c(s_{t},a_{\text{done}})$.
comments.explanation: The cost function assigns a task error when Done is called, but does not specify whether this cost is incurred once or repeatedly. If the agent can call Done multiple times—especially in a resettable environment—it may exploit this by terminating prematurely near the goal, incurring a small error, and restarting to minimize acting cost. This creates a loophole unless the cost function or environment dynamics prevent repeated termination. The paper should clarify that the task error is incurred only once per episode and that subsequent Done actions have no effect.

comments.title: Fixed operation policy confounds credit assignment
comments.quote: The pre-trained operation policy  $\hat{\pi}$  is fixed during the training and evaluation phases.
comments.explanation: The paper fixes $\hat{\pi}$ but does not address how its limitations affect learning of $\psi_\theta$. If $\hat{\pi}$ performs poorly on subgoals in unseen environments, $\psi_\theta$ may learn to avoid SUB actions not because they are unhelpful, but because they lead to failure due to $\hat{\pi}$'s inadequacy. This confounds credit assignment: poor performance could stem from either policy. The skyline experiment acknowledges this, but the main setup does not disentangle the contributions. The authors should report $\hat{\pi}$'s subgoal success rate or justify why its fixed nature does not bias $\psi_\theta$'s behavior.

comments.title: Subgoal path selection lacks specification
comments.quote: Specifically, it can ask for a description of its current location (Cur), the goal location (Goal), or a subgoal location that is on the path from its current location to the goal location (Sub).
comments.explanation: The definition assumes a unique path from current to goal location, but in graph-structured environments like Matterport3D, multiple shortest paths often exist. Without specifying which path is used, the subgoal location is not uniquely determined, undermining reproducibility. While the Appendix may contain details, the main text should state how subgoals are selected—e.g., via a deterministic shortest-path algorithm like Dijkstra’s with lexicographic tie-breaking—to ensure clarity and consistency.

comments.title: Misleading claim about imitation learning and knowledge limits
comments.quote: As discussed previously, naively mirroring human external behavior cannot enable agents to understand the limits of their knowledge. We teach the agent to understand its intrinsic needs through interaction with the human and the environment rather than through imitation of human behaviors.
comments.explanation: The claim that imitation learning cannot capture knowledge limitations is too strong. Recent work in uncertainty-aware imitation learning (e.g., Bayesian behavior cloning, active imitation) explicitly trains agents to detect distributional shift and request help when uncertain. Methods using dropout, energy-based models, or confidence thresholds allow imitative agents to recognize out-of-distribution states. The distinction should be reframed: HARI uses reinforcement learning to learn query timing and type, not that imitation is inherently incapable of modeling epistemic limits.

coarse__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.label: Coarse (qwen3.6-35b-a3b)
coarse__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.overall_feedback:
The paper proposes HARI, a hierarchical RL framework for learning when and what to ask a human assistant in a POMDP navigation setting. The core idea of combining a frozen low-level navigation policy with a learned interaction policy for information-seeking is intuitive and addresses a relevant gap in human-agent communication. The experimental results on Matterport3D demonstrate clear improvements over sparse baselines, and the analysis of action frequencies provides useful behavioral insights. However, the paper suffers from methodological bottlenecks in the hierarchy design, weak baseline comparisons, and an arbitrary cost formulation that requires stronger justification. Addressing these issues would significantly strengthen the empirical validation and theoretical framing.

The paper tackles an interesting problem at the intersection of hierarchical RL and human-agent communication. The formulation of a goal stack for managing subgoals and information requests is well-motivated, and the empirical evaluation on unseen navigation tasks shows promising results. The work successfully demonstrates that learning an interaction policy yields tangible benefits over static information strategies. Nevertheless, the current experimental setup and cost design limit the generalizability of the claims, and the lack of comparison to established hierarchical methods leaves the algorithmic contribution somewhat under-validated.

Overview issues:

- Frozen Low-Level Policy Creates a Distributional Mismatch Bottleneck: Section 5 explicitly states that the pre-trained operation policy $\\hat{\\pi}$ is fixed during the training and evaluation of the interaction policy $\\psi_\\theta$. While this decouples the learning stages, it introduces a severe distributional mismatch: $\\hat{\\pi}$ was trained on dense observations but must operate under sparse descriptions during evaluation. This mismatch likely caps the maximum achievable performance and makes it difficult to isolate the true contribution of the interaction policy. The paper acknowledges this limitation in Section 6 but does not propose a mitigation strategy or analyze how the hierarchy degrades as the observation gap widens. To validate the framework, the authors should either fine-tune $\\hat{\\pi}$ jointly with $\\psi_\\theta$ or provide a sensitivity analysis quantifying performance drops under varying observation sparsity. This will clarify whether the gains stem from the interaction policy or merely from compensating for a rigid low-level policy.

- Outperformance vs. Dense Oracle Lacks a Controlled Baseline: The abstract and Section 6 claim that the RL-learned agent outperforms the dense-description baseline on unseen environments (19.8% vs. 9.4%). This result is attributed to the ability to request subgoals, but the comparison is confounded by the fact that the dense baseline never changes its goal description. If the agent simply requested `Cur` at every step, it would effectively receive dense information continuously, yet the paper does not include this baseline. Without it, readers cannot determine whether the performance gain comes from hierarchical decomposition or simply from on-demand dense perception. The authors should add a baseline that requests dense descriptions at every time step. This control will rigorously separate the benefits of information density from the benefits of subgoal planning, which is central to the paper's claims.

- Missing Comparisons to Established Hierarchical RL Methods: The experimental evaluation in Section 6 relies heavily on a rule-based baseline and oracle information settings, but it omits direct comparisons to standard hierarchical RL architectures. Methods like the Options Framework, Feudal Networks, or HIRO have been extensively validated on navigation and POMDP tasks. Without benchmarking against these established approaches, it is unclear whether the proposed interaction policy offers a genuine algorithmic advancement or simply benefits from the specific reward shaping and cost design. The domain context explicitly highlights the availability of such baselines. Implementing and comparing against at least two standard hierarchical methods on the same Matterport3D task would contextualize the performance gains and validate the architectural choices made in Section 4.

- Arbitrary Cost Function and Failure Penalty Design: Section 4.4 and Section 6 define the task error for calling `Done` as the unweighted length of the shortest path to the goal. This formulation ties the penalty for early termination directly to environment geometry rather than treating failure as a distinct, high-cost event. Consequently, the agent's risk sensitivity is artificially shaped by path lengths, which may not reflect realistic scenarios where any premature termination is equally undesirable. The paper does not justify this design choice or analyze how the policy adapts to different failure cost magnitudes. Replacing the distance-dependent penalty with a fixed large cost for failure, or including a sensitivity analysis across multiple penalty settings, would strengthen the theoretical grounding and ensure the learned policy is robust to varying task priorities.

- Insufficient Analysis of Training Stability and Convergence: The paper uses Advantage Actor-Critic to train the interaction policy but provides minimal details on training dynamics, hyperparameters, or convergence behavior. Hierarchical RL is notoriously prone to training instability and credit assignment issues, particularly when managing a discrete goal stack and triggering a frozen low-level policy. The absence of learning curves, variance analysis across multiple seeds, or ablation on training stability leaves readers uncertain about the method's practical reliability. Adding training curves that show reward convergence and standard deviation, along with a discussion of hyperparameter sensitivity for the A2C implementation, is necessary for reproducibility. This analysis will demonstrate whether the framework consistently converges or relies on lucky initialization.

- Overgeneralization Claims vs. Task-Specific Instantiation: The abstract and conclusion frame HARI as a general POMDP extension for human-agent communication, yet the instantiation is tightly coupled to a discrete graph-based navigation task with a bag-of-features observation space. The paper does not demonstrate how the framework handles continuous state spaces, varying assistant reliability, or different communication modalities. The domain context notes that scalability to complex environments and modeling human variability are open challenges. The authors should explicitly delineate the general theoretical framework from the specific navigation instantiation. Adding a discussion or preliminary experiment showing how the framework adapts to continuous observations or noisy assistant responses, and tempering the generalization claims in the abstract to match the experimental evidence, would align the paper's scope with its actual contributions.

- Lack of a worked special case to calibrate policy behavior: The paper proposes a general POMDP extension with a hierarchical interaction policy, yet all analysis relies on a complex Matterport3D navigation task. Readers cannot easily calibrate intuitions about why the agent chooses to request a subgoal versus a state description without a simpler, fully-specified model. The authors should compute the interaction policy's behavior on a small, parametric example, such as a 2D gridworld with sparse versus dense observations and a known transition function. This would demonstrate concretely how the cost trade-offs and stack management lead to information-seeking decisions. Such a worked example would validate that the framework operates as intended rather than producing empirical artifacts of the navigation simulator.

- Missing discussion on handling high-dimensional description spaces: The framework defines a description space and a goal-description stack, but provides no guidance on how the interaction policy scales when these spaces become high-dimensional. In complex environments, conditioning on a growing stack of descriptions will quickly hit the curse of dimensionality. The paper does not discuss how to compress or retrieve information from the stack during training, nor does it address the computational feasibility of maintaining a belief over the stack. Adding a section on implementation strategies, such as attention mechanisms over the stack or recurrent memory updates, would bridge the gap between the theoretical formulation and practical deployment. Without this, the method's applicability to richer environments remains unverified.

- Underdeveloped link between framework generality and modality constraints: The abstract and introduction frame the framework as a general extension for human-agent communication capable of incorporating any form of information. However, the experimental instantiation is strictly limited to a discrete bag-of-features representation. The paper does not demonstrate how the operation policy must be architecturally adapted to process alternative modalities like natural language, continuous vectors, or symbolic rules. To deliver on the claim of framework generality, the authors should either provide a brief discussion on how the input spaces of the policies must be modified for different communication types, or include a small experiment showing the framework adapting to a text-based description modality. This would clarify the actual scope of the proposed architecture.

- Missing guidance on controlling stack depth during deployment: The paper demonstrates that deeper goal stacks boost performance in unseen environments but also notes that the agent over-requests subgoals due to critic overestimation. While the authors acknowledge this limitation, they provide no concrete strategy for controlling stack depth at deployment time. Readers will wonder how to prevent the agent from exhausting the stack with low-value requests in longer-horizon tasks. The authors should add an implementation discussion or ablation showing how to bound stack utilization, such as by learning a termination threshold or adjusting the subgoal action cost dynamically based on stack height. This would ensure the recursive capability is reliable rather than fragile.

Recommendation: Major revision is recommended. The paper addresses a relevant problem and presents a coherent hierarchical framework, but the experimental validation lacks necessary baselines and controls to support the stronger claims. The frozen low-level policy creates a distributional mismatch that bottlenecks performance, and the cost function design requires stronger justification. Addressing the missing hierarchical baselines, adding controlled information-density baselines, and providing training stability analysis are essential to establish the method's validity and generalizability.

Revision targets:

- Add a baseline that requests dense descriptions at every time step to isolate the benefits of subgoal decomposition from information density.

- Implement and compare against at least two standard hierarchical RL baselines (e.g., Options Framework or Feudal Networks) on the same task.

- Replace the distance-dependent failure penalty with a fixed cost or provide a sensitivity analysis across multiple penalty settings to justify the cost design.

- Include training curves, variance analysis across multiple seeds, and hyperparameter sensitivity results to demonstrate convergence and stability.
